stem: A pupil is choosing between writing a program in Python or in machine code.
model_answer: Python is a high-level language: it uses English-like keywords (if, while, def), one statement is translated into many machine instructions, and the same source code can run on any computer that has a Python interpreter. Machine code is very low-level: instructions are binary, each one runs directly on the CPU, and the code is specific to one CPU instruction set. High-level code is much faster to write and maintain; machine code can be smaller and faster but is hard to read and debug. For a pupil's quiz program, Python is the right choice — speed of development matters, and a modern CPU runs Python plenty fast.
review_notes: Teacher-marked. Award marks for: feature of high-level (English-like, translated), feature of low-level (CPU-specific, binary), one applied trade-off (development time vs runtime speed).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Compare a high-level language and machine code. Explain which the pupil should choose, and why.

mark_points.text: high-level: English-like keywords / portable / one line → many instructions
low-level: binary / CPU-specific / runs directly on the hardware
trade-off applied: high-level is faster to write but produces less compact code (or equivalent justified recommendation)
